constraints.analysis_summary: After round 1, we have no information. The secret code is completely unknown. All colors (typically 6 in Mastermind) are possible for each of the 4 positions, and duplicates are allowed.

After round 2, we know that exactly two of the five guessed colors {red, blue, green, yellow, white} are in the secret, and those two are not in the positions they were guessed. The other three guessed colors are impossible. All other colors remain possible. This combines with round 1 (no info) to give this single constraint.

After round 3, we have two possible color sets for the secret (assuming distinct colors, ignoring multiplicities). The secret contains either: (Case A) 1 color from {green,yellow,white}, 1 from {red,blue}, and 2 from {black,purple}; or (Case B) 2 from {green,yellow,white}, 0 from {red,blue}, and 1 from {black,purple}. All present colors are misplaced in both rounds 2 and 3 (i.e., not at the positions they were guessed). No color is confirmed present or absent with certainty. The total number of colors in secret (including duplicates) is still unknown (likely 4 or 5). No locked positions yet.

After four rounds, we have narrowed the possible distinct color sets to five candidates (see above). The secret has 5 positions. All present colors from rounds 2, 3, and 4 are misplaced, meaning they cannot occupy the positions they were guessed in those rounds. No locked positions have been identified. The exact composition (including duplicates) and arrangement remain unknown. There is no confirmed color or impossible color among the guessed set, as each color appears in at least one candidate.

After combining all rounds, we have narrowed the possible secret compositions to three consistent scenarios. The secret always contains white, black, and orange. Green is impossible. The missing color from round 5 is either purple (scenario A) or yellow (scenarios B and C). Additionally, scenarios B and C add either red or blue, respectively, making 5 distinct colors. In scenario A, one color (likely one of white, black, orange, or yellow) appears twice. The positional constraints from rounds 2-4 forbid each of these colors from certain positions. Round 5 provides one exact lock, but its identity is unknown. Further guesses are needed to pinpoint the exact set and arrangement.

After six rounds, the secret has 5 positions. Confirmed present colors: white, black, and orange. Green and blue are impossible. There are two possible color sets: Case A: {white, black, orange, yellow} with one duplicate (4 distinct colors); Case B: {white, black, orange, purple, red} (5 distinct colors). One color from round5 is at exactly its guessed position (locked). All other positions from rounds 2-6 are misplaced, providing numerous forbidden position constraints. Further deduction requires identifying the locked position and the duplicate (if any).

After round 7, we have narrowed the secret to four possible arrangements. All share the same set of colors: white, black, orange, yellow, with one duplicate. Red, purple, green, blue are impossible. The positional constraints from rounds 2-7 have been satisfied by all four candidates. A single further guess should distinguish between them.

After round 8, the secret's color multiset is confirmed: two oranges, one yellow, one white, one black. The exact arrangement is reduced to two possibilities: B: [black, white, orange, orange, yellow] or D: [white, orange, black, orange, yellow]. All other colors (green, blue, red, purple) are impossible. Positional constraints from previous rounds (especially round 5's exactly one correct, round 7's two correct) are satisfied by both candidates.
